summary.category: Consumer Exploitation and Protective Rights Framework
points: Consumers occupy weak market positions due to information asymmetry and power imbalance with sellers. Exploitation occurs through unfair weights, hidden charges, adulterated goods, false advertising, and market manipulation. The Consumer Protection Act 1986 (COPRA) protects six rights: right to safety (hazard protection), information (transparent product details), choice (freedom to select), seek redressal (grievance access), consumer education, and representation (voice in decisions affecting consumers).

summary.category: Legal Redressal System and Quality Protection
points: COPRA established a three-tier Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission system: District Commission (claims up to Rs 1 crore), State Commission (Rs 1-10 crore), National Commission (exceeding Rs 10 crore). Consumers can file complaints themselves without expensive lawyers. Quality certification marks (ISI, Agmark, Hallmark, FPO) help identify safe products; certification is mandatory for health-affecting products (LPG cylinders, packaged water, food colours, cement), ensuring public safety and product quality.

summary.category: Consumer Movement and Informed Consumer Practices
points: Consumer movement arose from organized efforts against exploitation, achieving government legal protection through COPRA (enacted 1986). India observes December 24 as National Consumers' Day. Over 2,000 consumer organizations operate nationally; consumer courts became accessible without expensive legal help. Being well-informed—checking labels, verifying quality marks, comparing prices, keeping receipts, understanding agreements, knowing grievance redressal procedures—enables effective consumer protection, fair market participation, and protection against exploitation.
